questions.prompt: Direct: "I am tired," she said. Choose the correct reported form:

options.text: She said that she is tired.
She told that she was tired.
She said that I was tired.
She said that she was tired.

questions.explanation: The reporting verb is past (said), so the present simple 'am' backshifts to past simple 'was'. The pronoun 'I' changes to 'she'. We use 'say that', not 'told that'.

questions.prompt: Direct: "I will help you," he said. Choose the correct reported form:

options.text: He said that he will help you.
He told that he would help me.
He said that he would help me.
He said that he would help you.

questions.explanation: 'will' backshifts to 'would'. The pronoun 'you' changes to 'me' (the listener becomes the reporter). We use 'say that', not 'told that'.

options.text: She told that she was ready.
She said me that she was ready.
She told me that she was ready.
She told that the test was easy.

questions.explanation: 'tell' always requires a personal object: tell + person + (that) + clause. 'She told me that she was ready' is the only correct pattern.

questions.prompt: Direct: "Are you ready?" she asked me. Choose the correct reported question:

options.text: She asked me if I was ready.
She asked me if was I ready.
She asked me if I am ready.
She asked if was I ready.

questions.explanation: Yes/no questions use 'if/whether'. Word order becomes normal (subject before verb): 'if I was ready'. The tense backshifts: 'are' → 'was'.

questions.prompt: Direct: "Don't run in the corridor!" the teacher said. Choose the correct reported command:

options.text: The teacher said not to run in the corridor.
The teacher told the students don't run in the corridor.
The teacher told the students not to run in the corridor.
The teacher asked don't run in the corridor.

questions.explanation: Negative commands are reported with 'tell + person + not to + infinitive'. 'The teacher told the students not to run in the corridor' is correct.

questions.prompt: Direct: "I can swim," she said. The time word 'now' in her speech would become:

options.text: then
today
here
tomorrow

questions.explanation: When time and place words shift to reported speech, 'now' becomes 'then'. Other shifts: today → that day, tomorrow → the next day, here → there.
